briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 14, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Efforts to Expand Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Various local governments across the country are making multifaceted efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society. Specifically, libraries in Yongin Special City are establishing 'Green Libraries' to promote carbon neutrality practices in daily life [11, 12, 13, 23, 29, 31, 32, 35], while cities like Gwangmyeong and Wonju are encouraging citizen participation by operating environmental education weeks and strengthening environmental education for youth [16, 22, 24, 30]. Furthermore, Cheongju City is incentivizing citizens' energy saving through the provision of carbon neutrality points [17, 33], and Ulsan City's Carbon Neutrality Support Center is attempting innovative approaches, such as launching the 'Carbon Inspiration Lab' [21]. At the corporate level, private sector efforts are also continuing, with Yokogawa initiating its 2050 supply chain carbon neutrality roadmap and achieving SBTi Net-Zero target certification [7].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: As the transition to renewable energy accelerates both domestically and internationally, Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) is demonstrating positive progress, driving grid innovation for renewable energy expansion and successfully securing project financing (PF) for a $500 million renewable energy project in Guam [39, 41]. Hanwha Solutions reported excellent performance in the second quarter, driven by the co-growth of its chemical and solar businesses, and domestic companies like Unittest are also making significant strides, with Unittest securing a 60.1 billion won solar power generation project [40, 43]. However, while challenges in innovating and standardizing renewable energy storage technologies are ongoing [42], there are also reports of the U.S. solar industry facing difficulties in its clean energy transition, hampered by 'red tape' in federal permit delays [44], underscoring the importance of policy support. Additionally, in Changwon, where the closure of coal-fired power plants is accelerating, a citizens' march was held, demanding the expansion of public renewable energy and guaranteed full employment for power plant workers [37].

sections.title: ⚠️ Escalating Climate Change Threats and the Urgency of Response
sections.content: Globally, threats from climate change are intensifying, with marine ecosystems particularly hard-hit. Ocean warming has directly impacted 'sea forests,' accelerating global restoration projects [8], and as global warming threatens coral reefs worldwide, research is underway to find heat-resistant 'super reefs' [47]. Changes in polar regions are also sounding alarm bells, with Antarctica yielding important lessons through explorations of iceberg graveyards related to climate change [48]. Furthermore, climate change and water scarcity have been identified as major challenges for future generations [49], and specific economic and social ripple effects are emerging, such as the tea industry in the Assam region facing threats to its future due to climate change [50]. Amidst these circumstances, there is a growing call for the swift finalization of regulations governing greenhouse gas emissions [10].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Action and the Rise of Denialism
sections.content: The international community continues its actions to address climate change. Acadia is combating climate change through its largest restoration project to date [45], and inter-state cooperation is active, with South Korea and Italy pursuing joint responses for a major renewable energy transition [38]. Policy interest also continues, with climate change legislation being one of the main topics of discussion in U.S. state legislative seat races [46]. However, a concerning report indicates that climate change denialism is resurfacing in Australia, with many voters gravitating towards politicians who support it [51]. This suggests a significant impediment to climate change response efforts and once again emphasizes the importance of communication and education based on scientific facts.

briefing.closing: This concludes today's climate briefing for June 14, 2026. We appreciate your continued interest and participation.

articles.title: Denial is back in vogue. As Australia leads climate talks, it’s beyond time we took the issue seriously
articles.description: It is unlikely that many voters are flocking to Pauline Hanson for her scientific insights – but that is where they are lining up, regardlessWant to get this in your inbox when it publishes? Sign up for the Clear Air Australia newsletter herePolitics is disconnecting from long-held assumptions at historic speed and no one knows where the great unhinging will take us. On the climate crisis, denial is back in vogue – depending on what the algorithm feeds you.One Nation’s surge in the polls suggest

summary.phenomenon: In Australia, as support for Pauline Hanson's One Nation party surges, climate change denial is back in vogue. Many voters are flocking to politicians who endorse climate change denial.
summary.cause: The revival of climate change denial appears to be influenced by the information algorithms feed to users.
summary.outlook: The article states that it is uncertain what consequences this political disconnection and the resurgence of climate change denial will bring in the future.
